content.full_text:
RICHARDS, WATSON & GERSHON
ATTORNEYS AT LAvv
THIRTY-EIGHTH FLOOI!^
"* "",r." ".". .-r-t-.i#fi
LOS ANGELES, CAUFORNIA 9OO7I -1469
(213) 6a€-a4a4
TELECOPTEE C2r3) 626-0073
March 28, 1991
RICHARO RICHATIDS
!l
EIGHAEENTH FLOOR
,IOI CAUFOFNIA STFEEI
(415) e55'3434
TELEoOPTER (1 1 6) 9AA-A722
R6876-001-
Scott Hallabrin, Esq
Acting General counsit
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 9 58 04 -08 07
Re: Ranch Palos Verdes Redevelopment Agency
Menber Douglas Hinchliffe
Dear Mr. Hal,labrin:
Rancho Palos verdes Redevel-opment Agency nember Douglas
Hinchliffe has requested that r obtain written advice from the
Fair Political Practices Commission concerning his ability to
participate i.n various Agency and city council- decisions. Agency
member Hinchliffe has reviewed this letter and agrees that it
correctly presents the facts and the guestions he woul,d 11ke
answered. I discussed the naterials in this }etter briefly with
Mark Iilorodoni today.
I. 
Facts
The Portuguese Bend, Abalone cove and Klondike canyon
landslides negatively iropact the southerly portion of Rancho
Palos verdes. The Portuguese Bend landslide, which started in
1956 and continues to this day, is the largest. 
The county of
Los Angel,es was ultirnately found to have created this fandsfide
by cornmencing construction of an extension of crenshaw Boulevard.
The city of Rancho Palos Verdes was incorporated in
L973. The Abalone Cove and Ktondike canyon landslides started in
l-978 and L980, respectively. 
The Abalone cove l-andslide is
westerly of the Portuguese Bend l-andslide and the Klondike
canyon landslide lies to the east.
In response to the two landslides, the city council
enacted moratoriums substantiaLly prohibiting construction in the
areas subject to the landslides. The City al-so sponsored State


Scott Hallabrin, Esq.
ltarch 28, 199L
Page 2
legislation to authorize the creation of landslide abatement
districts .
As a result of this legislation, the city established
the Abalone cove and Klondike canyon Landslide Abatement
Districts to construct and rnaintain irnprovements to stop the
landslides. The City Council provided the initial 
funds to
operate both districts, 
and a member of the city council served
on the initial 
governing body of each districts. 
Both districts
now operate independently.
The city also formed the Rancho PaLos Verdes
Redevelopment Agency and incl-uded all property impacted by the
landsl-ides in Project Area Number l-. The purpose of Project Area
Nunber f- is to elininate the blight caused by the landslides and
to construct landslide abatement improvements. The county of Los
Angeles commenced litigation, 
which a group of homeowners later
joined, to invalidate Project Area Number L.
A group of homeosrners in the Abalone Cove ]andslide
area filed a lawsuit against the county of Los Angel,es and the
city of Rancho Palos verdes for damages caused by the l-andslide.
The parties ultinately settled the litigation.
Pursuant to the settlement, the county of Los Angeles
provided certain funds to the plaintiffs. 
In addition, the
county agreed to dismiss its lawsuit challenging formation of
Project Area #1 and agreed to fonn an j-mprovenent district 
in the
Abalone cove area to fund construction of landslide abaternent
improvements .
The county also agreed to purchase up to $l-0 rnillion
worth of bonds to be secured by liens on the propertj.es included
in the assessnent district 
and to accept payrnent of tax increment
by the Agency to reduce the assessments. The agreement further
provided that the county wou1d, upon conpiecion of .rii Abaione
Cove irnprovements, establish another assessment district 
in the
Portuguese Bend area and purchase $5 rnillion worth of improvement
bonds to construct landslide abatement j.mprovernents in that area.
The County of Los Angel-es forrned the Abalone Cove
assessment district 
in December, L99o. During the months leadj,ng
up to formation of the assessment district, 
the Agency agreed
that all tax incrernent revenues received by the Agency would be
utilized to retire the Abalone Cove bonds. Tax revenue
projections prepared by the Agency indicate that there should be
sufficient revenues to retire the $10 rnill-ion lrorth of Abalone
cove bonds, but there would probably not be sufficient revenue
to retire the Portuguese Bend bonds for at least twenty years.
Therefore, unless property values increase far nore rapidly than


Scott Hall,abrin, rsq.
March 28, 1991
Page 3
projected, it is unlikely that the Portuguese Bend bonds will be
issued, if at all, within the next t$renty years.
counciLman Hinchliffe owns a home in the Portuguese
Bend c1ub. The Portuguese Bend club Homeowners I Association, of
which all oqrners of property in the area are members, orrrns the
streets and the beach in the Portuguese Bend cIub.
Councilman Einchliffers property is located more than
3500 feet fron the eastern-most boundary of the Abalone Cove
landslide. His propertlr is located approxirnately 600 fee+- fron
the nearest boundary of the Portuguese Bend landslide.
Councilman Hinchliffe's property is located within the boundaries
of the Klondike Canyon landslide abatement district, 
al-though it
has never been subject to landsLide activity. 
In addition, his
property is located within the boundaries of Project Area No. 1
of the Rancho Palos verdes Redevelopment Agency and is subject to
the development noratoriurn the City irnposed on the landsfide
area.
It is unlikely that abatement of the Abafone cove
landslide will have a material financial effect on the value of
councilman Hinchliffets residence. A11 geologic inforrnation the
city and the Agency have received indicate that the Abalone cove
and Portuguese Bend landslide, although adjacent to one another,
are separate and distinct. 
In addition, to the extent there
rnight possibly be a relationship to the two landslides, abatement
of the Abalone Cove landslide will have no irnpact on stopping
Portuguese Bend landslide, although it is conceivable that
stabilizing the Portuguese Bend landslide might facilitate
stabilization of Abalone cove.
Given the proxinity between the Portuguese Bend
landslide and !lr. Hinchliffe's property, abatement of that
landslide night have a material financial effect on the value of
Mr. Hinchliffe's property. As a result, Mr. Hincirliffe abstains
frorn City and Agency decisions directed toward abating the
Portuguese Bend landslide.
Ouestions Presented.
l-. 
May Agency l{enber Hinchliffe vote on matters necessary to
inplement the settlenent agreement? Such matters, from
which Aqency Menber Hinchl,iffe has been abstaining, have
included, and may in the future include, adopting a
resolution consenting to fornation of the Abal-one Cove
Assessnent District within the city of Rancho Palos Verdes
boundaries, authorizing the expenditure of funds to defend
the litigation 
filed by horoeowners against fornation of the
assessment district, 
and sirnilar decisions.


scott Eallabrin, Esq.
llarch 28, 1991
Page 4
2. 
May Agency Member Hinchliffe vote on specific projects, such
as constructj.on of dewatering lrel,1s, installation of
pezometers, preparation of engineering studies and related
expenditures to install the improvements to be funded by the
$l-0 nillion of Abalone Cove assessment district 
bonds?
3. 
ltay Agency tlember Hinchliffe participate in decisions to
construct sinilar improvements to abate the Portuguese Bend
l andsl ide?
Discussion:
Abatement of the Abalone cove landslide will- not affect
the value of DIr. Hinchliffers property. Mr. Hinchliffe has
abstained fron such decisions because the county vrill not
purchase the $5 million worth of Portuguese Bend assessment
district 
bonds until all Abalone Cove irnprovements have been
installed. 
In other words, the only reason councj-Imember
Hinchliffe might have a conflict of interest on AbaLone cove
landsl,ide abatement improvernents is that expenditure of Abalone
cove assessrnent district 
bond proceeds might increase the
possibility that the county of Los AngeLes wi.1l purchase bonds
for abating the Portuguese Bend landslide pursuant to the
settlement agreement. There is nothing inherent in the Abalone
Cove irnprovements thernselves, hohrever, in which councilman
Hinchliffe is financially interested.
Under the present circumstances, it is likely that it
will take the Agency at least five to six years to expend the $l-0
rnillion $rorth of Abalone cove bonds. Further, it is possible
that, given the tax increnent projections, the Agency wifl never
call on the County to issue the $5 mill-ion worth of Portuguese
Bend bonds because the City will probably never approve issuance
of those bonds unless sufficient tax increment could be projected
to retire the bonds and i.nsure that the homeowners will not be
obl,igated to pay their assessments. Based on the abo.re, it seens
very possible that any possible financial effect on Mr.
Hinchliffers property of the construction of landslide abatement
improvements in Abalone cove with the $10 rnillion assessment
district 
proceeds would be too rernote to give rise to a conflict
of interest.
Sirnilarly, the design and construction of nost
individual landslide abatement improvernents in the Portuguese
Bend area will have no financi.al effect on the vafue of
Councilnan Hinchliffers property. Many rnillions of dollars of
inprovernents will need to be constructed over many years before
the Portugtuese Bend landslide can be stabllized, if stabilization
can ever occur. Construction of any single improvement will not
result in stabilization of the landslide and, therefore, wiII
have no financial effect on councilnan Hinchliffe's property.


scott Hallabrin, Esq.
March 28, l-99 L
Paqe 5
To provide a specific exaulple of the types of
decisions before the Agency, the Cityts geologic experts have
recommended that certain drainage channels be installed in
Portuguese Bend canyon. These facilities 
are located rnore than
600 feet frorn councilman llinchliffers property. These
improveroents will not serve Councilman Einchlj.ffets property,
which is located in a different hratershed.
These inprovenents are but one of nany other
inprovernents that will need to be ccnstructed to stabilize the
landslide. 
The question with respect to Portuguese Bend
landslide improvements, therefore, is whether Mr. Hinchliffe can
parti.cipate in decisions to construct improvements which do not
directly serve t1r. Hinchliffe's property and in which he is not
financially interested j.f they are part of a larger long term
project which might have a financial, effect on hj-s property,
Please do not hesitate to contact ne if you have any
questions or need any additional facts.
very truly yours,
b-" M^*-7'
steven L, Dorsey I I\l
SLD: rp
cc: Douglas Hinchliffe
Carol W. Lyrrch
0280170
